Build an email with the market hours and location (if found), the weather summary, and a short Saturday shopping list pulled from market-staples.md filtered to the current season. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Saturday market notes'.
expectedOutput: A Saturday morning email with market hours, weather, and your usual seasonal shopping list.
customization: Edit market-staples.md per season, change the city, or drop the weather section if unwanted.

name: Holiday Gift List Builder
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Contacts/friends.md and /home/workspace/Contacts/family-context.md for shared interests and notes. Build a holiday gift planning sheet listing each person, a one line vibe summary, a target spend tier (under 25, 25 to 75, 75 plus) drawn from a 'tier' column, and three gift ideas grounded in their notes. Write the sheet to /home/workspace/Personal/gift-list-<year>.md and email a short preview via send_email_to_user with the file path and a count of people covered.
expectedOutput: A November 1 email pointing to a fully drafted holiday gift list grounded in your real contacts.
customization: Adjust spend tiers, expand to coworkers or neighbors, or move the kickoff date earlier.

name: Vercel Deploy Failure Sentinel
prompt: Run a bash curl against the Vercel API (https://api.vercel.com/v6/deployments) using $VERCEL_TOKEN to list the latest 10 deployments across configured projects. Parse JSON for any state of ERROR, CANCELED, or BUILD_ERROR newer than the last_seen timestamp in /home/.z/state/vercel-deploys.json. For each new failure, send one concise SMS via send_sms_to_user: '<project> <branch> failed at <time UTC>, see <inspectorUrl>'. Update last_seen. Skip projects with state READY.
expectedOutput: A near-realtime SMS only when a Vercel build actually fails, with the project, branch, and link.
customization: Filter to specific project IDs, change the polling interval, or add Slack delivery alongside SMS.

name: Supabase Row Count Drift Watch
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Projects/db-monitor/tables.yml for the list of Supabase tables to track. For each table, run a bash curl against the Supabase REST API using $SUPABASE_URL and $SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY with Prefer: count=exact and limit=0 to read the Content-Range header. Append today's row count per table to /home/workspace/Projects/db-monitor/counts.csv. Compute day-over-day delta. If any table dropped by more than 5 percent or grew by more than 200 percent, email a short report via send_email_to_user with subject 'Row count drift alert'.
expectedOutput: A morning email only when a tracked Supabase table shows an unusual day-over-day row count change.
customization: Tune the drift thresholds per table, add hourly counts, or pipe alerts to Discord.

name: Pre-Launch Checklist Verifier
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Projects/launch/checklist.yml, which lists checks: HTTPS reachable on production URL, /robots.txt returns 200, /sitemap.xml returns 200, og:image meta tag present in HTML, Plausible script tag present, primary form POST returns 2xx. Run each check via curl in bash. Build a markdown report with pass/fail per check, then email via send_email_to_user with subject 'Pre-launch checks: <pass count>/<total>'. Save the same report to /home/workspace/Projects/launch/last-report.md.
expectedOutput: A three-times-weekly email scoring your launch checklist against the live site.
customization: Add or remove checks in checklist.yml, change the cadence, or fail loudly with SMS on red checks.

name: Cron Last-Run Auditor
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Ops/cron-registry.yml, which lists each scheduled job by name with expected_max_gap_minutes and a heartbeat file path under /home/workspace/Ops/heartbeats/. Run bash 'stat -c %Y' on each heartbeat file to find the last modification time. Compute minutes since last run. For any job where minutes_since_last_run exceeds expected_max_gap_minutes, include it in an email via send_email_to_user with subject 'Stale cron jobs: <count>'. If nothing is stale, send nothing.
expectedOutput: A daily email only when one or more cron-style jobs missed their expected window.
customization: Update cron-registry.yml as you add jobs, or tighten the per-job allowable gap.

name: NPM Downloads Pulse
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Projects/oss/packages.yml for a list of npm package names you maintain. For each, curl https://api.npmjs.org/downloads/range/last-week/<package> and parse daily counts. Compute last-week total and week-over-week change vs the prior week. Append a row per package to /home/workspace/Projects/oss/npm-pulse.csv. Email a Monday summary via send_email_to_user with subject 'NPM weekly pulse' showing each package, last-week downloads, and percentage change as a small markdown table.
expectedOutput: A Monday morning email with weekly download counts and week-over-week changes for your npm packages.
customization: Add packages to the YAML, change the time window from last-week to last-month, or post to Discord.

name: Pull Request Aging Report
prompt: Use the gh CLI in bash to run 'gh pr list --repo <owner/repo> --state open --json number,title,author,createdAt,updatedAt,reviewDecision' for each repo listed in /home/workspace/Projects/oss/repos.txt. Compute days_open and days_since_update. Bucket PRs into 'fresh' (under 7 days), 'aging' (7 to 21 days), and 'stale' (over 21 days). Build a markdown report grouped by repo then bucket. Email via send_email_to_user with subject 'Open PRs by age, Friday review'.
expectedOutput: A Friday afternoon email grouping your open PRs by repo and age bucket.
customization: Filter to PRs needing your review, exclude draft PRs, or change the age bucket boundaries.

name: Stripe MRR Movement Digest
prompt: Run a bash curl against the Stripe API (https://api.stripe.com/v1/subscriptions?status=active&limit=100) using $STRIPE_SECRET_KEY. Paginate as needed. Sum recurring amount normalized to monthly to compute today's MRR. Read yesterday's MRR from /home/workspace/Finance/mrr-history.csv. Append today's row (date, mrr_cents, active_subs, new_today, canceled_today). Email a short digest via send_email_to_user with subject 'MRR <amount>, <delta>' showing the daily delta, new subscriptions, and cancellations.
expectedOutput: A morning email with current MRR, daily change, new signups, and cancellations.
customization: Switch to test mode keys, add ARR, or include churn rate from a 30 day window.

name: Cloudflare Cache Hit Rate Watch
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Ops/cloudflare-zones.yml for zone IDs to monitor. For each zone, run a bash curl against the Cloudflare GraphQL Analytics API using $CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN to fetch cache status counts over the last 6 hours. Compute hit_rate = hits / (hits + misses). Append rows to /home/workspace/Ops/cf-cache.csv. If any zone has hit_rate below 0.70 and request volume above 500, POST a JSON alert payload to $SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL summarizing the zone, the rate, and the volume.
expectedOutput: A Slack ping only when a Cloudflare zone's cache hit rate drops below 70 percent at meaningful volume.
customization: Adjust the hit-rate floor, the volume floor, or the polling interval per zone.

name: The Docs Drift Detector
prompt: For each repo in /home/workspace/Projects/oss/repos.txt, run bash 'gh api repos/<repo>/commits?per_page=30' to fetch recent commits. Read /home/workspace/Projects/oss/docs-paths.yml for per-repo code globs (src/**, lib/**) and docs globs (README.md, docs/**). Using git diff between HEAD and the last documented release tag, compute lines changed in code paths vs docs paths over the last 30 days. Flag repos where code lines changed exceeds 200 and docs lines changed is under 20. Email findings via send_email_to_user with subject 'Docs drift candidates'.
expectedOutput: A Tuesday email listing repos whose code has moved fast while docs have not.
customization: Tune the line thresholds, change the lookback window, or add per-repo overrides.

name: SEO Title Tag Auditor
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Projects/seo/pages.txt for URLs to audit. For each URL, run bash curl and parse the <title> and <meta name="description"> tags. Check: title length 40 to 60 characters, description 120 to 160, no duplicate titles across the list, no missing description. Append a row per page to /home/workspace/Projects/seo/title-audit.csv. Email a Monday report via send_email_to_user with subject 'SEO title audit, <issue count> issues' showing only the failing pages and the specific issue.
expectedOutput: A Monday email listing only the pages whose title or description tags fail length or uniqueness checks.
customization: Edit pages.txt, change length bounds, or add open graph image checks.

name: Discord Standout-Messages Pulse
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Projects/community/discord-channels.yml. For each tracked channel, run a bash curl against the Discord API (GET /channels/<id>/messages?limit=100) using $DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN to fetch the last week of messages. Compute messages_count, unique_authors, top 3 most-reacted messages. Append weekly rollups to /home/workspace/Projects/community/discord-pulse.csv. Email a Monday digest via send_email_to_user with subject 'Discord weekly pulse' summarizing each channel and quoting the top reacted message per channel.
expectedOutput: A Monday email summarizing message volume, active members, and standout messages per Discord channel.
customization: Add or drop channels, change the lookback window, or surface unanswered questions.

name: Founder Sleep Debt Tracker
prompt: Send an SMS via send_sms_to_user asking 'Hours slept last night? Reply with a number, decimals fine.' Append the reply with date to /home/workspace/Health/sleep.csv. Compute a 7 day moving average and a running sleep debt (target_hours - actual, summed over 7 days, target_hours from /home/workspace/Health/sleep-target.txt). If sleep debt exceeds 5 hours, send a second SMS: 'Sleep debt at <X> hours. Consider an early night.' Otherwise stay quiet.
expectedOutput: A morning text logging sleep and a follow-up only when your rolling sleep debt is high.
customization: Change the target hours, the debt threshold, or add a third nightly bedtime ping.

name: PostgreSQL Slow Query Spotter
prompt: Run a bash psql command against the database using $DATABASE_URL to query pg_stat_statements ordered by mean_exec_time desc limit 20, filtered to calls > 50 and mean_exec_time > 500. Diff results against /home/workspace/Ops/pg-slow-baseline.csv. For any queryid not in the baseline whose mean_exec_time is above 1000ms, POST a Slack alert to $SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL with the queryid, mean_exec_time, calls, and the first 200 chars of the query text. Update the baseline file at the end of each run.
expectedOutput: A Slack ping only when a newly slow Postgres query crosses one second average execution time.
customization: Adjust the time and call thresholds, narrow to specific schemas, or switch delivery to email.

name: Cold Outreach Reply Rate Tracker
prompt: Use use_app_gmail to search the 'Sent' label for messages with subject prefix 'Outreach:' from the last 14 days. For each, check whether a reply exists in the same thread. Compute total_sent, total_replied, and reply_rate. Read /home/workspace/Projects/outreach/campaigns.yml to attribute each sent message to a campaign based on subject tag (e.g. [c-spring]). Append weekly metrics per campaign to /home/workspace/Projects/outreach/reply-rates.csv. Email a Friday summary via send_email_to_user with subject 'Outreach reply rates, <overall>%' as a table.
expectedOutput: A Friday email with reply rates per cold-outreach campaign over the last two weeks.
customization: Change the subject tag scheme, the lookback window, or split by send weekday.

name: Logfire Error Spike Watcher
prompt: Run a bash curl against the Logfire query API using $LOGFIRE_READ_TOKEN to count error-level spans in the last 10 minutes across services listed in /home/workspace/Ops/logfire-services.yml. Read the previous 10 minute baseline from /home/workspace/Ops/logfire-baseline.json. If the current count exceeds max(20, baseline * 3), send a single SMS via send_sms_to_user: 'Error spike in <service>: <count> errors, baseline <baseline>'. Always update the rolling baseline as an exponential moving average.
expectedOutput: An SMS only when a tracked service crosses an error spike threshold relative to its rolling baseline.
customization: Tune the spike multiplier, the floor, or which services are watched.

name: Indie Hacker Comp Scan
prompt: Read /home/workspace/Projects/competitive/comps.yml, a list of competitor product names, X handles, and changelog URLs. For each, run web_search for '<product> changelog OR release' restricted to the last week, and run x_search for posts from the X handle in the same window. Summarize each competitor in 3 bullets (shipped, repositioned, claims). Email a Monday brief via send_email_to_user with subject 'Comp scan, week of <date>' as a markdown report grouped per product.
expectedOutput: A Monday email summarizing what each tracked competitor shipped or said publicly in the last 7 days.
customization: Add or remove competitors, change the time window, or weight by changelog vs social signals.

name: AWS Bill Anomaly Alert
prompt: Run a bash aws-cli call: 'aws ce get-cost-and-usage --time-period Start=<7daysago>,End=<today> --granularity DAILY --metrics UnblendedCost --group-by Type=DIMENSION,Key=SERVICE'. Parse JSON for per-service daily cost. Compute each service's 7 day average and yesterday's cost. Flag any service where yesterday is greater than max(2.0 dollars, 1.6 * 7 day average). Append daily totals to /home/workspace/Finance/aws-daily.csv. Email a flagged-services report via send_email_to_user with subject 'AWS spend anomaly, <count> services' only when at least one service flags.
expectedOutput: A morning email only on days when AWS spend for a service jumps well above its weekly baseline.
customization: Tune the multiplier, set a per-service floor, or include linked-account breakdowns.

name: The Open PR Bottleneck Map
prompt: For each repo in /home/workspace/Projects/oss/repos.txt, use the gh CLI to list open PRs with reviewers, requested reviewers, latest review state, and CI conclusion. Categorize each PR into exactly one bottleneck: 'waiting on author' (changes requested, no new push), 'waiting on review' (no review yet, CI green), 'CI broken', or 'merge conflict'. Build a markdown report grouping PRs by category across all repos with PR title, repo, author, and age in days. Email via send_email_to_user with subject 'PR bottlenecks: <total> open'.
expectedOutput: A twice-weekly email mapping every open PR to the specific reason it is not merging.
customization: Add labels to filter on, exclude bots, or send only one category (e.g. 'CI broken') as SMS.

name: Tomorrow's Outfit Weather Check
prompt:
You are running an evening outfit check for tomorrow.

1. Call web_search with the query: 'hourly forecast Salmon Idaho tomorrow' (one query, latest results).
2. From the results, pull: morning low, afternoon high, precipitation chance, wind, and any advisories.
3. Translate the forecast into a 2 sentence outfit suggestion. Mention layers, footwear, and whether to grab a coat or a hat. Be concrete (for example: 'Fleece plus shell, waterproof boots').
4. Send the result via send_sms_to_user. Keep it under 320 characters total. No greetings, no signoff.

Do not include any links. Do not echo the raw forecast numbers unless they matter for the outfit call.
expectedOutput: A short SMS at 9pm with tomorrow's forecast translated into a 2 sentence outfit call.
customization: Change the location in the search query, the send time, or swap SMS for Telegram.

name: The Lunch Break Forcer
prompt:
You are running the lunch break enforcer.

1. Call use_app_google_calendar with tool 'google-calendar-list-events' on the primary calendar for the window noon to 2pm local time today.
2. Find the longest contiguous free gap of at least 30 minutes in that window.
3. If a gap exists, send an SMS naming the exact start and end time of the gap and one specific lunch action (for example: 'Walk to the kitchen, eat the leftovers, no laptop').
4. If the entire window is booked, send an SMS that names the back to back meetings and suggests the user block 30 minutes after the last one.

Use send_sms_to_user. One message only. No emojis.
expectedOutput: An SMS naming a specific lunch window or flagging an overbooked midday.
customization: Adjust the lunch window, the minimum gap length, or the message style.

name: School Pickup Window Alert
prompt:
You are running the school pickup window alert.

1. Call use_app_google_calendar with tool 'google-calendar-list-events' on the primary calendar from now until 5pm today.
2. Call web_search for 'traffic conditions [city] afternoon' replacing [city] with the user's stored city if known, otherwise skip this step.
3. Estimate the latest moment the user should leave the house to reach the school by 3:15pm. Assume a 15 minute drive unless traffic results say otherwise.
4. Send an SMS with: the leave-by time, any calendar conflicts in that window, and one short note about traffic if relevant.

Use send_sms_to_user. Keep it under 280 characters.
expectedOutput: An SMS at 2pm naming the leave-by time and any conflict with that window.
customization: Change the pickup time, the assumed drive time, or the city in the traffic query.

name: Sunday Meal Prep Scout
prompt:
You are planning the user's meal prep for the coming week.

1. Call use_app_google_calendar to list events Monday through Friday this week.
2. Identify which weeknights have evening commitments after 5pm. Those nights need fast or pre-made dinners.
3. Call web_search for 'one pan dinner recipes 30 minutes' and pick 3 distinct results that are not duplicates of the last 4 weeks.
4. Build a meal plan: 2 prep-ahead lunches, 5 dinners (matched to the calendar), and one grocery list grouped by produce, protein, pantry.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with the subject 'Meal Plan: Week of [Monday date]'. Format the body in plain markdown with sections.
expectedOutput: A Sunday morning email with a weekday meal plan matched to the user's calendar plus a grouped grocery list.
customization: Swap the recipe style, change dietary constraints in the search query, or move the send day.

name: Library Book Due Date Tracker
prompt:
You are checking the user's library checkout log.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/library-checkouts.md'. If the file does not exist, send an email explaining the user needs to create that file with a markdown table of title, due date, renewals used.
2. Parse each row. Flag any book due within 4 days as URGENT, due within 10 days as SOON, and overdue as LATE.
3. For each LATE or URGENT row, suggest one of: renew, return today, finish tonight.
4. Send via send_email_to_user with the subject 'Library: X urgent, Y soon, Z late'.

If the table is empty, send a one line email saying the shelf is clear.
expectedOutput: A Wednesday email listing books due soon with a one word action for each.
customization: Change the file path, the urgency thresholds, or the day of the week.

name: The Quiet Hour Calendar Guard
prompt:
You are protecting the user's morning quiet hour, defined as 8am to 9am local time.

1. Call use_app_google_calendar to list events for today between 8am and 9am.
2. If any event lands inside that window, write a short note for each: title, organizer, whether it looks declinable (recurring team standup vs. one-off external).
3. If the window is clear, send a one line email: 'Quiet hour clear. Use it.'
4. If the window is taken, list each conflict and suggest a specific reschedule slot from the rest of the morning that is open.

Use send_email_to_user. Subject: 'Quiet hour status'.
expectedOutput: A 6am email confirming the 8 to 9 block is clear, or listing every conflict with a suggested move.
customization: Change the quiet hour window, the days it runs, or the delivery channel.

name: Birthday Card Address Audit
prompt:
You are checking the user's birthday list for the coming 3 weeks.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/birthdays.md'. Expect a markdown table with name, date (MM-DD), relationship, address known yes/no.
2. For each birthday falling in the next 21 days, compute the mail-by date as 5 calendar days before the birthday.
3. Group results into 3 buckets: 'Card this week', 'Card next week', 'Card week after'.
4. For anyone with address known = no, add them to a 'Need address' bucket.
5. Send via send_email_to_user. Subject: 'Birthday cards: [N] this week'.
expectedOutput: A Monday email grouping upcoming birthdays into mail-by buckets plus a missing-address list.
customization: Change the lead time, the file path, or the bucket structure.

name: The Friday Pantry Audit
prompt:
You are running the Friday pantry audit.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/pantry.md'. Expect a markdown list with item, quantity, last restocked date.
2. Flag items with quantity 'low' or last restocked over 90 days ago.
3. Identify items that pair into a meal (rice plus a protein plus a vegetable). Suggest 2 meals the user could make this weekend without shopping.
4. Build a shopping list of low items grouped by produce, dairy, dry goods.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Pantry: [N] low, [M] weekend meals possible'.
expectedOutput: A Friday evening email with a shopping list, low item flags, and 2 weekend meal options from existing stock.
customization: Change the file format, the staleness threshold, or the meal style.

name: Trash Day Night-Before Ping
prompt:
You are reminding the user about trash pickup tomorrow.

1. Determine which day of the week tomorrow is.
2. Call web_search for 'holiday trash pickup schedule [city] this week' replacing [city] with the user's city if known. If no holiday delay is found, assume the regular schedule.
3. If tomorrow is a regular pickup day, send an SMS: 'Trash out tonight. [Recycling/Compost] too if applicable.'
4. If a holiday delay shifted the schedule, send an SMS naming the new pickup day.

Use send_sms_to_user. One message. No emojis. Under 200 characters.
expectedOutput: An 8pm text the night before pickup with a one line reminder and any holiday shift.
customization: Change the schedule, the city, or which bins (recycling, compost, yard waste) get mentioned.

name: Vet Vaccine Booster Watcher
prompt:
You are checking the dog's vaccine schedule.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/dog-health.md'. Expect a list of vaccines with last administered date and interval (in months).
2. For each vaccine, compute the next due date as last + interval.
3. Flag any vaccine due within the next 60 days, and any overdue.
4. For each flagged row, write one sentence the user can paste into a vet office message to book the appointment.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Dog vaccines: [N] due in 60 days, [M] overdue'.
expectedOutput: A monthly email listing vaccines coming due or overdue with copy-paste vet office text for each.
customization: Change the lead window, the file format, or extend it to cover cats or other pets.

name: Dog Park Air Quality Check
prompt:
You are checking whether it is safe to take the dog to the park today.

1. Call web_search for 'AQI [city] today' replacing [city] with the user's city if known, otherwise default to 'Salmon Idaho'.
2. Read the current AQI value and category from the top result.
3. If AQI is under 100, send an SMS: 'Air clear ([value]). Long walk is fine.'
4. If AQI is 100 to 150, send: 'Moderate ([value]). Short walk, water break.'
5. If AQI is over 150, send: 'Skip the park ([value]). Indoor play today.'

Use send_sms_to_user. One message. Include the numeric value.
expectedOutput: A 7am SMS with a one line walk recommendation based on today's AQI.
customization: Change the AQI thresholds, the city, or the activity (run, hike, bike).

name: Doctor Appointment Prep Brief
prompt:
You are building a prep brief for any medical appointment tomorrow.

1. Call use_app_google_calendar to list events tomorrow tagged with 'doctor', 'dentist', 'vet', 'PT', 'clinic', or 'appt'.
2. If none, exit silently (do not send an email).
3. For each matching event, call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/health-log.md' to pull the latest 3 entries.
4. Build a brief per appointment: provider name, address, arrival time (15 min before), what to bring (insurance card, ID, medication list), 3 questions to ask based on recent log entries.
5. Send via send_email_to_user. Subject: 'Tomorrow [appointment type] prep'.
expectedOutput: An evening email with a per-appointment prep brief, sent only if there is a medical appointment tomorrow.
customization: Change the tag list, the log file, or the arrival buffer.

name: The Refrigerator Cleanout Reminder
prompt:
You are sending the weekly fridge cleanout reminder.

1. Pick a rotating focus area: shelf 1 (top shelf, leftovers), shelf 2 (drinks), shelf 3 (produce drawers), shelf 4 (condiments and door). Rotate weekly based on the week number of the year (week mod 4).
2. Send a single SMS naming this week's focus area and 2 specific things to check (for example for produce drawers: 'soft tomatoes, slimy greens').
3. Add one suggested action: toss, use tonight, or compost.

Use send_sms_to_user. Under 200 characters. No greeting.
expectedOutput: A Thursday evening SMS naming the week's fridge zone and 2 specific items to inspect.
customization: Change the rotation, the zones, or the send day to align with trash pickup.

name: Plant Watering By Species
prompt:
You are deciding which plants need water today.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/plants.md'. Expect a list of plants with species, last watered date, and watering interval in days.
2. For each plant, compute days since last watered. If days since last >= interval, it is due today.
3. If 0 plants are due, exit silently.
4. If 1 to 3 are due, send an SMS naming each by location (for example: 'Kitchen pothos, bedroom snake plant').
5. If 4 or more are due, send: '[N] plants due. Open plants.md.'

Use send_sms_to_user. Remind the user to update the file after watering.
expectedOutput: A morning SMS only when plants are actually due, naming them by location.
customization: Adjust the file format, add a notes column for fertilizer schedule, or split by indoor/outdoor.

name: Holiday Gift Shopping Countdown
prompt:
You are running the holiday gift countdown.

1. If today's month is not October, November, or December, exit silently.
2. Compute days until December 24.
3. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/gift-list.md'. Expect a markdown table with recipient, idea, status (idea / bought / wrapped / shipped).
4. Count: total recipients, ideas locked, bought, wrapped, shipped. Compute gap.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Holiday gifts: [days] days, [bought]/[total] handled'. Body: the gap broken down by recipient, plus the 3 recipients with no idea yet.

If the file does not exist, send an email explaining the format needed.
expectedOutput: A Monday morning email through Q4 with days remaining, gift progress, and 3 recipients still missing ideas.
customization: Move the target date (Hanukkah, birthdays), change the file path, or run year-round.

name: Family Group Chat Highlights
prompt:
You are summarizing the week in family communications.

1. Call use_app_gmail to search for emails from the user's family contacts in the last 7 days. Use the query 'from:(family OR mom OR dad OR sister OR brother) newer_than:7d' as a starting point. The user maintains a family-tag label, so also search 'label:family newer_than:7d'.
2. For each thread, write a one sentence summary including who, what, and whether a reply is owed.
3. Group results: 'Reply owed', 'FYI only', 'Action by you'.
4. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Family week: [N] reply owed, [M] FYI'.

State plainly which threads the user has not replied to.
expectedOutput: A Sunday evening email grouping the week of family threads into reply-owed, FYI, and action buckets.
customization: Change the Gmail label, add specific names, or expand to cover close friends.

name: The Mortgage Rate Watch
prompt:
You are tracking 30-year fixed mortgage rates for a possible refinance.

1. Call web_search for 'current 30 year fixed mortgage rate this week' and pull the headline national average.
2. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/mortgage-rate-history.md'. If absent, treat history as empty.
3. Append today's date and rate to the history file via create_or_rewrite_file (preserve prior rows).
4. Compare today's rate to: the user's current rate (assume stored in the file header as 'Current rate: X.XX%'), 4 weeks ago, and the 12 week trend.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Mortgage rate: [X.XX%] today'. Include a recommendation: 'No action', 'Monitor', or 'Call broker' based on whether today's rate is more than 0.5 points below the user's current rate.
expectedOutput: A Tuesday morning email with today's mortgage rate, a 12 week comparison, and a refinance action call.
customization: Change the rate type (15 year, ARM), the threshold for action, or the source query.

name: Credit Card Statement Quick Read
prompt:
You are summarizing the most recent credit card statement.

1. Call use_app_gmail with a search query for 'subject:(statement OR statement available) from:(chase.com OR americanexpress.com OR capitalone.com OR discover.com OR citi.com) newer_than:14d'.
2. For each statement email found, extract: card name, statement period, total charges, minimum due, due date, any flagged unusual spending if mentioned.
3. Pull the largest 5 line items if visible in the email body.
4. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Cards this cycle: [N] statements, $[total] due'. Group by card.

Do not open any links. Do not download attachments. Just read what is in the email body.
expectedOutput: A monthly email with statement totals, due dates, and the largest 5 charges per card.
customization: Add or remove issuers, change the lookback window, or include rewards balance summaries.

name: The Inbox Promo Sweep
prompt:
You are sweeping the promotions tab.

1. Call use_app_gmail with the query 'category:promotions newer_than:7d'.
2. Group by sender domain.
3. Identify the top 5 senders by message count.
4. For each top sender, suggest one of: 'Unsubscribe', 'Keep (you opened 2+ this week)', 'Keep (recent purchase)'. Use whatever signal the email metadata gives you.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Promo sweep: [N] senders, [M] unsubscribe candidates'. List the top 5 with a one line recommendation each.

Do not unsubscribe automatically. Just report.
expectedOutput: A Friday email naming the top 5 promo senders this week with a one line keep/unsubscribe call for each.
customization: Change the lookback window, the number of senders, or expand to the updates tab.

name: Tax Document Gathering Nudge
schedule: 0 9 15 1,2,3 * (9am on the 15th of January, February, March)
prompt:
You are nudging the user through tax document collection.

1. Call use_app_gmail with the query 'subject:(1099 OR W-2 OR 1098 OR tax document OR tax form) newer_than:90d'.
2. List every distinct sender and document type found.
3. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/tax-checklist.md' (expect a list of expected forms with status).
4. Match received documents against the checklist. Compute which forms are still missing.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Tax docs: [received]/[expected] received'. Body: a clean list of received with date and a clean list of still missing.

If today is March 15 and any documents are missing, mark the message URGENT in the subject.
expectedOutput: Three nudges per tax season comparing inbox documents against an expected-forms checklist.
customization: Change the months it runs, add more form types to the search, or edit the checklist.

name: The Pharmacy Refill Lookahead
prompt:
You are running the medication refill lookahead.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/medications.md'. Expect a list with med name, last filled date, days supply.
2. For each med, compute the run-out date as last filled + days supply.
3. Flag any med running out in the next 10 days as 'Refill this week'.
4. Flag any med running out in 11 to 21 days as 'Refill within 2 weeks'.
5. If any meds need refill this week, send a single SMS naming each one. If none, exit silently.

Use send_sms_to_user. Keep the message under 300 characters.
expectedOutput: A Monday morning SMS listing meds needing refill this week, sent only when something is actually due.
customization: Change the warning windows, the file path, or extend to OTC supplies.

name: Concert Ticket Drop Alert
prompt:
You are watching for tour dates from artists the user cares about.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Documents/concert-watch.md'. Expect a list of artists and a list of acceptable cities or regions.
2. For each artist, call web_search with the query '[artist] tour dates 2026' (one query per artist, sequential).
3. Match any announced dates against the user's acceptable cities or regions.
4. Build a list of matching shows: artist, city, venue, date, on-sale date.
5. If matches exist, send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Concert watch: [N] matches'. If none, exit silently.

Do not buy tickets. Just report.
expectedOutput: A Wednesday email listing newly matched tour dates in the user's regions, only when there are matches.
customization: Change the watch list, the city list, or expand to comedy and sports.

name: The Thank You Note Queue
prompt:
You are surfacing thank you notes the user owes.

1. Call use_app_gmail with the query 'newer_than:14d (gift OR helped OR thanks OR thank you OR favor)'.
2. From the threads, identify ones where someone did something for the user (received a gift, got a referral, got hosted, got a recommendation).
3. For each, write a 2 sentence thank you note draft the user can copy.
4. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Thank you queue: [N] notes owed'. Include the recipient, the reason, and the draft per row.

Do not send any thank yous yourself. The user copies and sends manually.
expectedOutput: A Monday email with a queue of thank you note drafts ready to copy and send.
customization: Change the search lookback, add a Notion database, or split into 'card' and 'text' columns.

name: School Closure Snow Day Check
prompt:
You are checking for school closures or delays today.

1. If today's month is not November through March, exit silently.
2. Call web_search for 'school closures delays [school district name] today'. The district name is stored in '/home/workspace/Documents/school-district.md'. Read it first.
3. Also call web_search for 'winter storm warning [county] today'.
4. If either search shows a closure, delay, or active storm warning, send an SMS with: status (closed / 2 hour delay / on schedule), reason if known, and one sentence on road conditions.
5. If no closure or warning is found, exit silently.

Use send_sms_to_user. One message. Under 280 characters.
expectedOutput: A 5:30am SMS only on days when school is delayed, closed, or a storm warning is active.
customization: Change the months it runs, the district, or add bus route specifics.

name: The Garage Sale Saturday Scanner
prompt:
You are scouting garage sales for today.

1. Call web_search for 'garage sales [city] this weekend' and 'estate sales [city] this weekend' (two queries, parallel).
2. From the results, extract listings with address, hours, and a short note about what is being sold if mentioned.
3. Filter to sales starting before noon.
4. Group by neighborhood if addresses make that clear.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Garage sales: [N] today'. Include a suggested route hitting 3 to 5 stops by neighborhood proximity.

If no sales are found, send a one line email: 'Nothing posted this week.'
expectedOutput: A Saturday morning email listing garage sales by neighborhood with a suggested 3 to 5 stop route.
customization: Change the city, the cutoff time, or expand to flea markets and swap meets.

name: The Morning Postmortem Drafter
prompt:
You are drafting postmortem skeletons for any incident closed in the last 24 hours.

1. Call use_app_linear to list issues in the 'Incidents' project closed in the last 24 hours.
2. If none, exit silently.
3. For each incident, call use_app_gmail to find related alert emails (search 'newer_than:2d ([incident title] OR [issue ID])').
4. Draft a postmortem markdown file at '/home/workspace/Projects/postmortems/[YYYY-MM-DD]-[issue-id].md' with sections: Timeline, Detection, Customer impact, Root cause (TBD), Action items (TBD).
5. Use create_or_rewrite_file to write each draft.
6. Send a single summary email via send_email_to_user listing the drafts created with their file paths.
expectedOutput: A daily file drop with postmortem skeletons for incidents closed yesterday, plus an email index, sent only when incidents occurred.
customization: Change the Linear project, the section template, or the lookback window.

name: Stripe Subscription Churn Pulse
prompt:
You are summarizing last week's subscription churn from Stripe.

1. Call run_bash_command to query the Stripe API for subscriptions that moved to status canceled or past_due in the last 7 days. Use the STRIPE_SECRET_KEY environment variable. Command pattern: 'curl -s -u $STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: "https://api.stripe.com/v1/subscriptions?status=canceled&created[gte]=$(date -d \'7 days ago\' +%s)&limit=100"'.
2. Parse the JSON. For each canceled sub, pull: customer email, plan name, MRR contribution, cancellation reason if present.
3. Also pull past_due subscriptions and flag the ones older than 5 days as at risk.
4. Compute total MRR lost this week and a 4 week trend if a prior log exists at '/home/workspace/Projects/stripe/churn-log.md'.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Churn week: $[MRR lost], [N] canceled, [M] past due'.
expectedOutput: A Monday email with last week's churned subs, MRR impact, and past-due accounts at risk.
customization: Change the lookback, add segment breakdowns by plan, or switch to test mode for staging.

name: The Open PR Decay Report
prompt:
You are reporting on stale pull requests across the user's repos.

1. Call run_bash_command with 'gh pr list --search "is:open updated:<$(date -d \'14 days ago\' +%Y-%m-%d)" --json number,title,repository,author,updatedAt,reviewDecision --limit 100' to list PRs open more than 14 days.
2. Group by repository.
3. For each PR, write a one line decay status: 'Awaiting review', 'Changes requested ignored', 'No CI run', 'Ready to merge stuck'.
4. Suggest one action per PR: ping reviewer, close, rebase, merge.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'PR decay: [N] PRs stuck > 14 days'. Body grouped by repo.

If zero stale PRs, send a one line email confirming the queue is clean.
expectedOutput: A Wednesday email grouping stale PRs by repo with a decay status and one-action call per PR.
customization: Change the staleness threshold, scope to one org with --owner, or add Linear ticket cross-links.

name: Vercel Build Failure Forensics
prompt:
You are watching Vercel deployments for failures.

1. Call run_bash_command with 'curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $VERCEL_TOKEN" "https://api.vercel.com/v6/deployments?state=ERROR&limit=20"' to fetch recent errored deployments.
2. Filter to deployments that errored in the last 35 minutes (just after the previous run).
3. For each, fetch the build log via 'curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $VERCEL_TOKEN" "https://api.vercel.com/v2/deployments/[id]/events"' and pull the last 30 lines.
4. Extract the first error line and the file path mentioned, if any.
5. If any new failures, send via send_discord_message with the project name, branch, commit SHA, first error line, and a link to the deployment.

If no new failures, exit silently.
expectedOutput: A Discord ping within 30 minutes of a Vercel build failing, with the first error line and link.
customization: Change the interval, narrow to specific projects, or switch to Slack delivery.

name: The Cold DM Warmer
prompt:
You are warming up cold outbound targets on X before the user pitches.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Projects/outbound/targets.md'. Expect a list of X handles with status (cold / warming / contacted).
2. For each handle with status 'cold' or 'warming', call x_search with allowed_x_handles=[handle], time_range='week' to pull their last 7 days of posts.
3. For each handle, identify the post most relevant to the user's offering (the file header lists the pitch context).
4. Draft a 1 sentence reply the user can post that is specific to the post content and not generic.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Outbound warm-up: [N] reply drafts'. Include the post URL and the draft reply per target.

Do not post anything. The user posts manually.
expectedOutput: A 3-day-a-week email of specific reply drafts for cold outbound X targets.
customization: Change the cadence, add LinkedIn targets, or auto-rotate which handles are reviewed.

name: ICP Signal Scraper
prompt:
You are scanning for buying-intent signals in the user's ICP.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Projects/icp/signals.md'. Expect a list of trigger phrases (for example: 'looking for', 'tired of', 'switching from', '[competitor name] alternatives').
2. For each trigger phrase, call x_search with the phrase as the query, time_range='week'.
3. Also call web_search for the phrase scoped to reddit.com and indiehackers.com (two searches per phrase).
4. Filter results to posts that mention the user's ICP traits (also stored in the signals.md header).
5. Build a ranked list of the top 10 signals: source link, signal type, post excerpt, suggested outreach angle.
6. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'ICP signals: [N] this week'.
expectedOutput: A Tuesday email with the top 10 buying-intent signals from X and Reddit matching the user's ICP.
customization: Edit the trigger phrases, add Hacker News, or change the ICP filter criteria.

name: Bug Bash Hot Spot Map
prompt:
You are mapping the bug hot spots in the codebase based on the last 30 days.

1. Call use_app_linear to list issues labeled 'bug' closed in the last 30 days.
2. For each, parse any file paths or component names mentioned in the issue description or comments.
3. Aggregate by file path or component. Find the top 10 hot spots by bug count.
4. Call run_bash_command to count git commits touching each hot spot file in the last 30 days, and also count test files paired with each one.
5. Write the report to '/home/workspace/Projects/quality/hot-spots-[YYYY-MM].md' via create_or_rewrite_file.
6. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Bug hot spots: top 10 for [month]' and the file path.
expectedOutput: A monthly markdown report identifying the top 10 buggy files plus an email link.
customization: Change the labels considered, the lookback window, or add severity weighting.

name: The Customer Quote Miner
prompt:
You are mining the inbox for quote-worthy customer language.

1. Call use_app_gmail with the query 'newer_than:7d (loved OR finally OR exactly OR thank you OR saved OR helped) -label:promotions'.
2. From each thread, extract any sentence written by a customer that praises a specific outcome (not generic 'great service').
3. For each extracted quote, capture: customer name, company if known, quote text, the specific outcome.
4. Append the quotes to '/home/workspace/Projects/marketing/customer-quotes.md' via create_or_rewrite_file (preserve existing entries; add a dated section).
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Quote mine: [N] new this week'. Include the top 3 quotes by specificity in the body.

Do not invent quotes. If none qualify, send 'No new quotes this week.'
expectedOutput: A Friday email plus an appended quote file with new specific customer praise lines.
customization: Change the search terms, add a permission-to-use checkbox column, or sync to Notion.

name: Newsletter Open Rate Triage
prompt:
You are triaging the user's newsletter performance after each send.

1. Call use_app_gmail with the query 'from:(noreply@substack.com OR mailchimp.com OR convertkit.com OR ghost.io) subject:(stats OR open rate OR report) newer_than:14d'.
2. From the most recent stats email, extract: post title, open rate, click rate, total subscribers, unsubscribes.
3. Compare each metric to the prior 4 send average if mentioned in the email body.
4. Write a triage call: 'Subject line worked', 'Subject line flopped', 'Topic fatigue', 'Healthy growth'.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Newsletter triage: [post title]'. Include the metrics and the triage call.

If no new stats email exists, exit silently.
expectedOutput: A Tuesday email diagnosing the most recent newsletter send with a one line triage call.
customization: Add specific providers, change the comparison baseline, or pipe results to a Notion table.

name: The Pricing Page Diff Watcher
prompt:
You are watching competitor pricing pages for changes.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Projects/competitive/pricing-urls.md'. Expect one URL per line.
2. For each URL, call run_bash_command with 'curl -sL -A "Mozilla/5.0" [url] | grep -iE "\\$|/mo|/month|per user|free|pro|enterprise" | sed -E "s/<[^>]+>//g" | tr -s " " | head -50' to extract pricing-relevant text.
3. Compare the output to the prior snapshot at '/home/workspace/Projects/competitive/snapshots/[domain].txt'.
4. If a diff exists, save the new snapshot via create_or_rewrite_file and capture the changed lines.
5. If any competitor changed pricing, send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Pricing diff: [N] competitors moved'. Include diff lines per competitor. If none changed, exit silently.
expectedOutput: A daily email only on days when a tracked competitor's pricing page text actually changed.
customization: Edit the URL list, change the grep pattern, or alert via Discord instead.

name: SOC2 Evidence Collector
prompt:
You are collecting weekly SOC2 evidence artifacts.

1. Call run_bash_command to capture: (a) 'gh api /user/repos --paginate | jq \'.[] | select(.archived==false) | {name, default_branch, default_branch_protected: .protected}\'' for branch protection status, (b) 'gh api /orgs/[org]/audit-log --paginate' if the org name is in '/home/workspace/Projects/compliance/org.md', (c) a list of currently active user sessions in any system you have shell access to.
2. Save the raw outputs as dated files in '/home/workspace/Projects/compliance/evidence/[YYYY-Www]/' via create_or_rewrite_file.
3. Upload the folder contents to Google Drive via use_app_google_drive into a folder named 'SOC2 Evidence/[YYYY-Www]'.
4. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'SOC2 evidence: week [N] collected'. Body lists each artifact and its Drive link.
expectedOutput: A Friday email plus dated evidence files in workspace and Google Drive for weekly SOC2 collection.
customization: Add or remove evidence sources, change the storage location, or run monthly instead.

name: The Sentry Noise Suppressor
prompt:
You are recommending which Sentry issues to ignore or auto-resolve.

1. Call run_bash_command to query the Sentry API: 'curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN" "https://sentry.io/api/0/organizations/[org]/issues/?statsPeriod=7d&query=is:unresolved&limit=100"'. The org slug is in '/home/workspace/Projects/sentry/org.md'.
2. For each issue, capture: title, event count, user count, first seen, last seen, level, culprit.
3. Flag as 'Noise candidate' any issue with: events > 1000, user count = 1 (same user only), or culprit matching a known third-party script.
4. Flag as 'Probable bug' any issue with: rising trend (more events in the last 3 days than the prior 4) or user count > 10.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Sentry triage: [noise] to ignore, [bugs] to chase'. List each with a one line recommendation.
expectedOutput: A Monday email separating last week's Sentry issues into noise to ignore and real bugs to chase.
customization: Change the noise rules, the lookback, or scope to specific projects.

name: Twitter Algorithm Posting Window
prompt:
You are finding the best posting window on X for today based on recent engagement.

1. Call x_search with allowed_x_handles=['jeffkazzee'], time_range='month' to pull the user's posts from the last 30 days.
2. For each post, capture: timestamp (hour of day in local time) and engagement (replies + reposts + likes).
3. Compute median engagement per hour-of-day bucket. Identify the top 2 hour buckets.
4. Send an SMS naming today's 2 best posting windows (for example: '9am to 10am, 7pm to 8pm') and one note about which type of post performs best in each window (text, image, link).

Use send_sms_to_user. Under 280 characters. No emojis.
expectedOutput: A 7am SMS naming today's two best windows to post on X based on the last 30 days.
customization: Change the handle, the lookback, or extend to LinkedIn or Bluesky.

name: The Product Hunt Launch Scout
prompt:
You are scouting yesterday's Product Hunt launches.

1. Call web_search for 'site:producthunt.com posts [yesterday's date YYYY-MM-DD]'. Also try 'Product Hunt top launches yesterday'.
2. From results, identify the top 10 launches by upvotes.
3. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Projects/competitive/keywords.md'. Expect a list of categories or keywords the user tracks.
4. Filter the 10 launches to ones matching the user's keywords. If fewer than 3 match, include the next-best by upvote count to reach 5 total.
5. For each, write: name, one sentence on what it does, why it matters to the user (or 'background noise'), product URL.
6. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'PH scout: [N] relevant launches'.
expectedOutput: A daily email with 5 Product Hunt launches filtered to the user's tracked keywords.
customization: Edit the keyword file, change the upvote threshold, or expand to Hacker News Show HN.

name: Stale Branch Garbage Collector
prompt:
You are identifying stale Git branches the user could delete.

1. Call run_bash_command with 'gh repo list --json name --limit 100 | jq -r \'.[].name\'' to list the user's repos.
2. For each repo, call 'gh api repos/[user]/[repo]/branches --paginate | jq -r \'.[] | select(.name != "main" and .name != "master") | .name\''.
3. For each non-default branch, call 'gh api repos/[user]/[repo]/branches/[branch] --jq \'.commit.commit.author.date\'' to get last commit date.
4. Flag branches with no commits in 60+ days and no open PR.
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Stale branches: [N] across [M] repos'. Group by repo. Include the suggested 'gh' command to delete each branch (but do not run it).

Do not delete anything. The user reviews and runs the commands manually.
expectedOutput: A Monday email listing stale branches across repos with copy-paste delete commands.
customization: Change the staleness threshold, exclude specific repos, or include branch protection status.

name: SEO Position Drift Report
prompt:
You are tracking the user's search ranking for tracked keywords.

1. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Projects/seo/keywords.md'. Expect a list of keywords plus the target URL (the user's domain).
2. For each keyword, call web_search with the keyword as the query. Scan the top 20 results for the target URL.
3. Capture position (or 'not in top 20') for each keyword today.
4. Append today's row to '/home/workspace/Projects/seo/position-history.csv' (date, keyword, position) via create_or_rewrite_file.
5. Compare today's positions against last Thursday's. Flag movements of 3+ spots in either direction.
6. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'SEO drift: [N] up, [M] down'. List the biggest moves with one possible cause each (new content from the user, competitor change, algorithm shift).
expectedOutput: A Thursday email with SEO position changes week over week for tracked keywords.
customization: Edit the keyword list, change the scan depth, or add competitor URLs to track.

name: The Investor Update Skeleton
prompt:
You are drafting the monthly investor update skeleton.

1. Call use_app_linear to list issues closed in the past calendar month tagged 'shipped' or 'milestone'.
2. Call run_bash_command to pull last month's MRR from Stripe: 'curl -s -u $STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: "https://api.stripe.com/v1/subscriptions?status=active"' and sum recurring amounts.
3. Call read_file on '/home/workspace/Projects/metrics/last-month.md' to compare against prior MRR if logged.
4. Build a markdown draft at '/home/workspace/Projects/investor-updates/[YYYY-MM].md' with sections: TL;DR (3 bullets), Numbers (MRR, growth, churn TBD), Shipped (from Linear), Hard things (TBD by user), Asks (TBD by user).
5. Use create_or_rewrite_file.
6. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Investor update draft: [Month]' and the file path.
expectedOutput: A monthly file drop with an investor update skeleton plus an email pointing to the draft.
customization: Add cashflow from QuickBooks, change the section structure, or run weekly instead.

name: Discord Community Pulse Check
prompt:
You are checking the pulse of the user's Discord community.

1. Call get_discord_history for each channel ID listed in '/home/workspace/Projects/community/channels.md', with limit=200, covering the last 7 days.
2. For each channel, count: total messages, unique authors, longest thread, any unanswered questions (messages ending in '?' with no reply within 4 hours).
3. Flag the top 3 unanswered questions across all channels.
4. Identify any new member who has not yet been welcomed (first message in the last 7 days with no @mention reply).
5. Send via send_email_to_user with subject 'Community pulse: [N] unanswered, [M] new members'. Include the questions verbatim and the new member handles.

Do not reply automatically. The user replies manually.
expectedOutput: A Friday email with unanswered questions and new-member welcomes the user owes for the week.
customization: Edit the channel list, change the response window threshold, or add a hot-topic summary.
